events.text: The patient is a 55-year-old female named Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo. She has a past medical history of hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine) and a 10 pack-year smoking history (quit 20 years prior). She works as a landscape architect and has significant sun exposure history. She is of mixed Afro-Caribbean and Eastern European descent.

At age 55 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) cutaneous malignant melanoma, superficial spreading type, arising from the left posterior shoulder. Primary lesion measured 4.2 mm Breslow thickness, Clark level V, with ulceration present and 4 mitoses/mm2. Relevant biomarkers included: BRAF V600E mutation positive; PD-L1 expression 45% (tumor proportion score); LDH 387 U/L (elevated, 1.5x upper limit of normal). Histology code: 8720/3 (Melanoma, NOS, malignant). Site code: C44.5 (Skin of trunk). Summary stage: Distant (Stage IV). Sites of metastatic disease at diagnosis: multiple subcutaneous nodules (right flank, left axilla), bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, and hepatic metastases (4 lesions, largest 3.1 cm in right lobe). ECOG performance status: 1. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY.

At age 55 years, the patient had a PET-CT (whole body, 18F-FDG) at the time of initial staging workup. The study showed: (1) hypermetabolic primary lesion on left posterior shoulder (SUVmax 8.2), (2) bilateral hypermetabolic axillary lymph nodes (right axilla: 2.1 cm, SUVmax 11.4; left axilla: 1.8 cm, SUVmax 9.7), (3) multiple hypermetabolic subcutaneous nodules in the right flank (up to 1.4 cm, SUVmax 6.8) and left axillary region, (4) four hepatic lesions with FDG avidity (right lobe: 3.1 cm SUVmax 14.2, 1.8 cm SUVmax 10.1; left lobe: 1.2 cm SUVmax 7.9, 0.9 cm SUVmax 6.3), and (5) no evidence of brain, bone, or pulmonary metastases. Impression: Widespread metastatic melanoma, M1c disease. Cancer status: present, metastatic.

At age 55 years, the patient had a pathology result from excision of the primary left posterior shoulder lesion and a core needle biopsy of the right axillary lymph node. Primary lesion: Invasive superficial spreading melanoma, Breslow thickness 4.2 mm, Clark level V, with ulceration, 4 mitoses/mm2, no regression, lymphovascular invasion present, perineural invasion absent, surgical margins clear (deep margin 4 mm, peripheral margins all >5 mm). Lymph node biopsy: Metastatic melanoma in right axillary lymph node, largest deposit 18 mm, with extranodal extension. Immunohistochemistry: S100 positive, SOX10 positive, HMB-45 positive, Melan-A positive, Ki-67 approximately 35%.

At age 55 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for metastatic cutaneous melanoma based on a specimen obtained from a right axillary lymph node core biopsy (FFPE), using a comprehensive solid tumor panel (523-gene panel). Clinically actionable alterations: BRAF p.V600E mutation (VAF 48%, pathogenic, tier 1), CDKN2A homozygous deletion (copy number loss, pathogenic, tier 1), PTEN p.R130Q mutation (VAF 31%, pathogenic, tier 1 - associated with reduced sensitivity to MEK inhibition). Additional somatic alterations: TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 39%, tier 2), RAC1 p.P29S mutation (VAF 12%, tier 3), PREX2 frameshift p.T935fs (VAF 22%, tier 3), ARID2 p.Q444* nonsense (VAF 19%, tier 3), MAP2K1 wild-type, NRAS wild-type, NF1 wild-type. Copy number alterations: CDKN2A homozygous deletion (chr9p21.3), MYC amplification (8q24, 6 copies), MITF gain (3q14). No clinically significant fusions detected. Tumor mutational burden: 42 mutations/Mb (high). Microsatellite status: MSS (microsatellite stable). PD-L1 IHC (companion diagnostic): 45% TPS. Germline: BRCA1/2 wild-type, CDK4 wild-type.

At age 55 years, the patient had a new patient oncology consultation. She presented with a rapidly growing subcutaneous nodule on the right flank and palpable bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy. She reported 8 lb unintentional weight loss over 2 months, mild fatigue (ECOG 1), but no fever, night sweats, or abdominal pain. Physical exam notable for a 1.5 cm firm, mobile subcutaneous nodule right flank, bilateral firm axillary lymphadenopathy. Labs: LDH 387 U/L (elevated), CBC and CMP otherwise within normal limits except mild transaminase elevation (ALT 52, AST 48). Given Stage IV melanoma with BRAF V600E mutation, positive PTEN loss, and elevated LDH indicating high tumor burden, decision made to initiate dabrafenib 150 mg BID plus trametinib 2 mg daily as first-line therapy given rapid disease kinetics and need for rapid tumor burden reduction. Plan for restaging imaging at 8 weeks. Discussion of future immunotherapy sequencing. Cancer status: present, with active disease.

At age 55 years, the patient received first-line targeted therapy with dabrafenib 150 mg orally twice daily plus trametinib 2 mg orally once daily (dabrafenib/trametinib combination; BRAFi/MEKi doublet) for Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma. Treatment intent: palliative/disease control. Cycle start date corresponds to approximately 3 weeks after diagnosis confirmation.

At age 55 years (approximately 3 weeks after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient developed grade 1-2 adverse events attributed to targeted therapy: pyrexia (temperature 38.4 degrees C, grade 1-2, managed with dose interruption of dabrafenib x3 days and acetaminophen), fatigue (grade 1), and a mild maculopapular rash on bilateral forearms (grade 1, managed with topical triamcinolone). Dabrafenib was resumed at full dose after fever resolution. No dose reduction required at this time.

At age 55 years (8 weeks after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for restaging. Results showed: marked interval decrease in size and number of subcutaneous nodules (right flank nodule now 0.3 cm, previously 1.4 cm); bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy significantly decreased (right axilla 0.7 cm, previously 2.1 cm; left axilla 0.5 cm, previously 1.8 cm); hepatic metastases with interval decrease in size (right lobe: 1.2 cm, previously 3.1 cm; 0.6 cm, previously 1.8 cm; left lobe: near-complete resolution of previously noted lesions); no new sites of disease identified; no brain metastases on MRI brain obtained same day. Impression: Partial response (PR) by RECIST 1.1 criteria. Estimated 62% reduction in target lesion sum. Cancer status: present, responding to therapy.

At age 55 years (8-week restaging), the patient had an oncology office visit. She reported significant symptomatic improvement, weight gain of 5 lbs, improved energy. Right flank nodule no longer palpable. LDH normalized to 198 U/L. LFTs within normal limits. Continued on dabrafenib/trametinib at full dose. Discussed excellent partial response. Noted mild hand-foot syndrome (grade 1) and ongoing fatigue (grade 1). Plan to continue therapy with restaging at 16 weeks. Counseled on signs of progression and importance of adherence. Cancer status: present, responding.

At age 55 years (approximately 4 months into dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient developed a second episode of pyrexia (temperature 39.1 degrees C, chills, rigors; grade 2) requiring emergency department visit. Infectious workup negative (blood cultures, urinalysis, chest X-ray). Attributed to dabrafenib-related pyrexia. Dabrafenib held for 5 days with resolution of fever. On resumption, dabrafenib dose reduced to 100 mg BID (first dose reduction), trametinib continued at 2 mg daily. Also noted grade 2 peripheral edema managed with elevation and sodium restriction.

At age 55 years (approximately 6 months after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for interval restaging. Results showed: continued stability/slight further decrease in residual axillary lymphadenopathy (right axilla 0.5 cm, left axilla 0.4 cm); residual hepatic lesions stable (right lobe: 1.0 cm and 0.5 cm); subcutaneous nodules not visualized; MRI brain: no evidence of intracranial metastases. Impression: Continued partial response, now best response achieved, with approximately 68% reduction from baseline target lesion sum. No new lesions. Cancer status: present, stable/responding.

At age 55-56 years (approximately 8 months after starting dabrafenib/trametinib), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for interval restaging. Results showed: interval increase in the right axillary lymph node (now 1.6 cm, previously 0.5 cm), new left axillary lymph node (1.1 cm), interval growth of hepatic lesions (right lobe: 2.3 cm, previously 1.0 cm; 1.4 cm, previously 0.5 cm), a new right lobe hepatic lesion (0.8 cm), and two new small pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 0.6 cm and 0.4 cm), not previously identified. MRI brain: no intracranial metastases. LDH 512 U/L (elevated). Impression: Progressive disease (PD) by RECIST 1.1 criteria. New hepatic lesion and pulmonary nodules represent new organ involvement. Cancer status: present, progressive disease.

At age 56 years (8-month restaging demonstrating progression), the patient had an urgent oncology visit. She reported new right upper quadrant discomfort, mild dyspnea on exertion, and weight loss of 6 lbs over 4 weeks. Labs: LDH 512 U/L (elevated), mild transaminase elevation (ALT 78, AST 65), otherwise normal. Discussion of progression on dabrafenib/trametinib. Given the BRAF V600E mutation-positive disease with acquired resistance to BRAFi/MEKi, and high TMB (42 mut/Mb) and PD-L1 45% suggesting potential immunotherapy sensitivity, decision made to transition to second-line ipilimumab (1 mg/kg) plus nivolumab (3 mg/kg) every 3 weeks x4 doses, then nivolumab maintenance. Discussed immune-related adverse events in detail with patient and family. Dabrafenib/trametinib discontinued. Baseline thyroid function (TSH 2.1), cortisol, and CBC reviewed. ECOG PS: 1. Patient agreed to proceed. Cancer status: present, progressive.

At age 56 years, the patient received second-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg IV plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction doses (CheckMate-067 dosing schema), for Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma with progression on dabrafenib/trametinib. Treatment intent: palliative with curative potential. Followed by planned nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance.

At age 56 years (during cycle 3 of ipilimumab/nivolumab, approximately 6 weeks after initiating combination immunotherapy), the patient developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis. She presented to the emergency department with 10-12 watery/bloody bowel movements per day, severe abdominal cramping, and fever (38.6 degrees C). Labs: CRP 94 mg/L, fecal calprotectin markedly elevated, hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL. Infectious colitis ruled out (C. difficile negative, stool culture/ova and parasites negative, CMV colitis not identified on biopsy). Flexible sigmoidoscopy showed diffuse moderate-severe colitis from rectum to descending colon, consistent with immune-related colitis. Ipilimumab and nivolumab both held. Patient admitted for IV fluids and initiated on high-dose IV methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg/day. Gastroenterology consulted.

At age 56 years (hospitalization for grade 3 colitis, inpatient day 4), the patient had an inpatient oncology consult note. She showed improvement after 3 days of IV methylprednisolone - bowel movements decreased to 4-5/day, no blood, abdominal pain improved. Transitioned to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with planned slow taper over 6-8 weeks per ASCO immunotherapy toxicity guidelines. Ipilimumab permanently discontinued given grade 3 toxicity. Plan for nivolumab rechallenge after complete steroid taper and colitis resolution, given PD-L1 45% and high TMB. GI follow-up arranged. Patient discharged on hospital day 5 on oral prednisone 80 mg daily, omeprazole, and calcium/vitamin D supplementation. Cancer status: present, but unable to assess treatment response during toxicity management.

At age 56 years (approximately 10 weeks after initiating ipilimumab/nivolumab, following grade 3 colitis, during steroid taper), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for response assessment. Results showed: interval decrease in right axillary lymphadenopathy (0.9 cm, previously 1.6 cm), left axillary lymphadenopathy decreased (0.6 cm, previously 1.1 cm), hepatic lesions with interval decrease (right lobe: 1.4 cm, previously 2.3 cm; 0.7 cm, previously 1.4 cm; new lesion no longer identified), and previously noted pulmonary nodules decreased in size (right lower lobe: 0.3 cm and 0.2 cm). MRI brain: no intracranial disease. LDH 241 U/L (normalizing). Impression: Partial response to immunotherapy. Cancer status: present, responding to prior immunotherapy.

At age 56 years (approximately 12 weeks after cycle 1, following complete steroid taper and colitis resolution confirmed on repeat flexible sigmoidoscopy), the patient was rechallenged with nivolumab monotherapy 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance immunotherapy for metastatic melanoma, after permanently discontinuing ipilimumab due to grade 3 colitis. The decision to rechallenge with nivolumab was supported by partial response seen on CT imaging and high TMB. Prednisone taper completed prior to nivolumab resumption (final dose of prednisone was <10 mg/day at rechallenge).

At age 56 years (first nivolumab monotherapy maintenance dose), the patient had an oncology visit. She reported resolution of diarrhea (1-2 formed bowel movements daily), no abdominal pain, and weight recovery (+4 lbs). Colitis in clinical remission. TSH checked: 6.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated, new hypothyroidism, likely immune-related thyroiditis). Levothyroxine dose increased from 75 mcg to 100 mcg. Labs: LDH 228 U/L (approaching normal), CBC normal, LFTs normal. ECOG PS: 1. Patient tolerating nivolumab rechallenge well. Bowel prep protocol discussed in case of future colonoscopy. Plan for monthly nivolumab and restaging every 12 weeks. Cancer status: present, partial response maintained.

At age 56 years (approximately 6 weeks into nivolumab maintenance), the patient developed grade 1-2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism (TSH 9.2 mIU/L, free T4 0.7 ng/dL). Managed with further levothyroxine dose increase to 125 mcg daily. No nivolumab dose interruption required. Patient advised to monitor for symptoms of hypothyroidism. Endocrinology referral placed for ongoing thyroid management. This was attributed to immune-related endocrinopathy, a known class effect of checkpoint inhibitor therapy.

At age 56 years (approximately 6 months into nivolumab maintenance, 9 months from initiation of combination immunotherapy), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast plus MRI brain for restaging. Results showed: right axillary lymph node 0.5 cm (stable/near-normal), left axillary lymph node 0.3 cm (near-normal), hepatic lesions with further interval decrease (right lobe: two lesions now measuring 0.6 cm and 0.3 cm, previously 1.4 cm and 0.7 cm), pulmonary nodules now below CT resolution (not visualized), and no new sites of disease identified. MRI brain: no intracranial metastases. LDH 178 U/L (normal). Impression: Deepening partial response on nivolumab maintenance, approaching near-complete response in liver. Cancer status: present, durable partial response with ongoing response deepening.

At age 56 years (6-month restaging on nivolumab maintenance), the patient had an oncology office visit. She reported good quality of life, ECOG PS 0, returned to full-time work, no diarrhea recurrence, well-controlled hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 125 mcg. Labs: LDH 178 U/L (normal), TSH 2.8 mIU/L (well-controlled), CBC and CMP normal. Imaging showing deepening partial response. Discussed ongoing nivolumab maintenance - per current evidence (CheckMate-067 long-term data), recommendation to continue nivolumab for at least 2 years total. Patient very engaged in care, enrolled in survivorship clinic. Discussed bowel surveillance protocol given prior colitis. Cancer status: present, durable partial response.

At age 57 years (approximately 12 months into nivolumab maintenance, 15 months from initiation of combination immunotherapy), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast plus MRI brain for restaging. Results showed: bilateral axillary lymph nodes within normal size limits (right axilla 0.4 cm, left axilla 0.3 cm), residual right hepatic lesion 0.4 cm (previously 0.6 cm, near-complete response), second hepatic lesion not identified, no pulmonary lesions identified, no new sites of disease. MRI brain: no intracranial disease. LDH 162 U/L (normal). Impression: Near-complete response (nCR) by RECIST 1.1, with only a single residual sub-centimeter hepatic lesion. Cancer status: minimal residual disease, near-complete response.

At age 57 years, the patient had an image-guided core needle biopsy of the residual right hepatic lesion (0.4 cm) to assess treatment response and viability. Pathology report: Liver core biopsy showing fibrotic/necrotic tissue with no viable melanoma cells identified. Scattered pigment-laden macrophages and hemosiderin deposition consistent with treated/regressed melanoma. Immunohistochemistry: HMB-45 negative, Melan-A negative, S100 focally positive in reactive stroma only. Impression: No viable tumor identified; findings consistent with complete pathologic response at this site.

At age 57 years (pathologic complete response confirmed at hepatic site), the patient had an oncology visit. Excellent news - biopsy of residual hepatic lesion shows no viable tumor. Radiographic near-complete response with pathologic confirmation. Discussed treatment duration: plan to continue nivolumab for total of 2 years from initiation of combination immunotherapy per protocol, then consider planned treatment holiday with close surveillance. Patient in excellent health, ECOG PS 0, working full-time, no active immune-related adverse events (hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine). Cancer status: no viable cancer identified at biopsied site; overall near-complete radiographic response.

At age 57 years (approximately 18 months into nivolumab maintenance, 2 years total from combination immunotherapy initiation), nivolumab was electively discontinued after completing 2 years of checkpoint inhibitor therapy (per CheckMate-067 and emerging evidence supporting treatment holiday after deep response). Final dose of nivolumab 480 mg IV administered. Patient transitioned to active surveillance with quarterly CT imaging and MRI brain every 6 months. Levothyroxine continued indefinitely. No systemic anti-cancer therapy ongoing at this time.

At age 57-58 years (approximately 6 months after completing nivolumab, 2.5 years from start of combination immunotherapy), the patient had a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast plus MRI brain for surveillance. Results showed: no axillary lymphadenopathy, no hepatic lesions identified (previously 0.4 cm lesion no longer seen), no pulmonary nodules, no subcutaneous disease, no new sites of disease identified. MRI brain: no intracranial metastases. LDH 155 U/L (normal). Impression: Complete radiographic response maintained 6 months after treatment holiday. Cancer status: no evidence of disease (NED).

At age 58 years (6-month post-treatment surveillance visit), the patient had an oncology follow-up visit. She is doing exceptionally well, ECOG PS 0, no symptoms of recurrence. Complete radiographic response confirmed. Labs: LDH 155 U/L (normal), CBC normal, CMP normal, TSH 3.1 mIU/L (stable on levothyroxine 125 mcg). Discussed survivorship: continued quarterly CT surveillance for first year off therapy, then transition to every 6 months. Skin surveillance with dermatology every 6 months ongoing (risk of new primary melanoma). Discussed signs and symptoms of recurrence. Patient expressing gratitude; strong engagement in survivorship. Hypothyroidism remains only long-term immune-related sequela. Cancer status: no evidence of disease.

documents.text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

INDICATION: 55-year-old female with known Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma, primary lesion left posterior shoulder, with metastatic involvement of bilateral axillary lymph nodes, subcutaneous nodules, and hepatic parenchyma. Currently receiving targeted therapy with dabrafenib plus trametinib (BRAFi/MEKi combination). This examination is obtained for interval restaging following prior partial response documented on CT at 8-week timepoint. Patient has had a recent episode of dabrafenib-related pyrexia requiring dose interruption and subsequent dose reduction of dabrafenib to 100 mg twice daily; trametinib maintained at 2 mg daily.

TECHNIQUE:
CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis: Helical acquisition was performed from the thoracic inlet through the pubic symphysis following administration of 100 mL of low-osmolar iodinated intravenous contrast (Omnipaque 350) with arterial and portal venous phase acquisitions. Oral contrast was administered. Images were reconstructed at 3 mm axial slices with multiplanar coronal and sagittal reformats. Dose modulation employed.

MRI Brain: Multiplanar multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner including axial T1, T2, FLAIR, DWI/ADC, T2* GRE (susceptibility-weighted), and pre- and post-gadolinium T1-weighted sequences (0.1 mmol/kg gadobutrol IV). Thin-slice axial and coronal post-contrast T1 sequences included for leptomeningeal evaluation.

Comparison: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained approximately 8 weeks prior.

---

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS:

THORAX:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No focal parenchymal consolidation, mass, or nodule is identified above the threshold of CT resolution. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No endobronchial lesion.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinal and hilar contours are normal in configuration. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy is identified. No anterior mediastinal mass. The thymus is not enlarged for age. No pericardial effusion. The esophagus is unremarkable in caliber.

Cardiovascular: The heart is normal in size. The aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilation or dissection. The pulmonary vasculature is normal. No evidence of central pulmonary embolism on this non-PE protocol examination.

Chest Wall and Pleura: No chest wall mass or destructive osseous lesion. No pleural thickening or nodularity. The visualized soft tissues of the thoracic inlet and bilateral axillae are evaluated below.

Bilateral Axillae: There has been continued interval decrease in the previously noted bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, in keeping with ongoing treatment response.
  - Right axillary region: The dominant lymph node previously measured 0.7 cm in short axis on the 8-week restaging examination (down from 2.1 cm at baseline). On the current examination, this node measures approximately 0.5 cm in short axis and appears slightly more reniform in morphology compared with the prior rounded, bulging appearance. No additional enlarged right axillary nodes are identified.
  - Left axillary region: The previously noted left axillary lymph node, which measured 0.5 cm at 8 weeks (down from 1.8 cm at baseline), now measures approximately 0.4 cm in short axis. It retains a thin fatty hilum, approaching normal morphology. No additional enlarged left axillary nodes.
  - Impression: Bilateral axillary lymph nodes have continued to decrease in size since the 8-week restaging examination, representing continued treatment response beyond what was documented at that time.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and contour without gross capsular retraction. The hepatic parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous enhancement. There are two residual low-attenuation foci in the right hepatic lobe, corresponding to previously identified metastatic lesions. These lesions appear mildly smaller compared with the 8-week CT examination and substantially smaller compared with baseline.
  - Right lobe lesion 1 (previously 1.2 cm at 8 weeks, 3.1 cm at baseline): Now measures approximately 1.0 cm in greatest dimension. Demonstrates peripheral rim enhancement with central hypoattenuation, morphologically consistent with a partially treated/necrotic lesion.
  - Right lobe lesion 2 (previously 0.6 cm at 8 weeks, 1.8 cm at baseline): Now measures approximately 0.5 cm. Demonstrates minimal residual enhancement. Margins are less distinct compared with baseline.
  - Left lobe: The two lesions previously identified at baseline in the left hepatic lobe remain not visualized on the current examination, consistent with near-complete or complete resolution of left lobe disease, as was suggested on the 8-week scan.
  - No new hepatic lesions are identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts are normal in caliber.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is normal in size without cholelithiasis or wall thickening. The common bile duct is not dilated.

Pancreas: Normal in size, contour, and parenchymal attenuation without evidence of mass, ductal dilation, or peripancreatic inflammatory change.

Spleen: Normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No focal splenic lesion. No perisplenic fluid.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology. No adrenal mass or enlargement. No adrenal metastases identified.

Kidneys and Ureters: The kidneys are normal in size and symmetrically enhancing. No hydronephrosis or perinephric stranding. No renal mass identified. No ureteral pathology visualized.

Gastrointestinal Tract: The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel are normal in caliber and wall thickness without evidence of obstruction, perforation, or mural thickening. No mesenteric or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy above the threshold of detection.

Retroperitoneum: No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber.

PELVIS:

Pelvic viscera: The uterus is normal in size and morphology. No adnexal mass. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. The urinary bladder wall is smooth without filling defect.

Bone Windows: Visualized osseous structures of the thoracic and lumbar spine, pelvis, and proximal femora are within normal limits without evidence of lytic or sclerotic lesions to suggest osseous metastatic disease.

Subcutaneous and Soft Tissues: No subcutaneous nodules are identified in the right flank, left axillary region, or elsewhere in the visualized soft tissues. The previously noted right flank subcutaneous nodule (0.3 cm on 8-week scan, down from 1.4 cm at baseline) is no longer resolved below the threshold of visualization.

---

MRI BRAIN:

Brain Parenchyma: The cerebral and cerebellar parenchyma are normal in signal intensity on all sequences. No areas of restricted diffusion to suggest acute or subacute infarction. No signal abnormality on FLAIR to suggest edema, demyelinating disease, or leptomeningeal infiltration. The cortical gray-white matter differentiation is preserved.

Post-Contrast Enhancement: Following administration of intravenous gadolinium, there is no abnormal parenchymal, meningeal, or leptomeningeal enhancement. No ring-enhancing or nodular enhancing lesion identified in the cerebral hemispheres, cerebellum, or brainstem. No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease.

Ventricles and Sulci: The ventricular system is normal in size and configuration without hydrocephalus. The sulci are normal in caliber and distribution for the patient's stated age.

Extraaxial Spaces: No subdural or epidural collection. No extra-axial mass. No herniation.

Calvarium and Skull Base: No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesion. No skull base erosion.

Orbit and Sella: Grossly normal orbits. The sella is normal in configuration without sellar mass.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Continued interval decrease in bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy since the 8-week restaging examination, with the right axillary node now measuring 0.5 cm and the left 0.4 cm - both approaching the upper limits of normal size. The degree of response from baseline is substantial, with nodes having decreased from 2.1 cm and 1.8 cm, respectively.

2. Residual right hepatic lobe metastatic foci show further but modest reduction in size compared with the 8-week scan (1.0 cm and 0.5 cm, respectively), with morphologic features of treatment response including central necrosis and reduced enhancement. Left hepatic lobe lesions remain absent, consistent with complete resolution at these sites.

3. Subcutaneous nodules previously identified at the right flank and left axillary region are no longer visualized, consistent with complete resolution of subcutaneous disease.

4. No new sites of disease are identified throughout the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.

5. No intracranial metastatic disease on MRI brain.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with ongoing, durable treatment response to targeted therapy (dabrafenib/trametinib), representing the deepest response achieved thus far in this treatment course. The pattern of continued disease reduction - though at a slower rate than the initial dramatic response seen in the first 8 weeks - is characteristic of sustained BRAF/MEK inhibition. Residual hepatic disease remains present in the right lobe and represents the dominant remaining site of measurable disease.

CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST
MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

INDICATION: 55-year-old female with known stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma, currently on dabrafenib 100 mg BID plus trametinib 2 mg daily (first-line targeted therapy, approximately 8 months of treatment). Presenting for interval restaging evaluation. Prior imaging demonstrated marked partial response at 8-week restaging and continued partial response at 6-month restaging (best response approximately 68% reduction in target lesion burden). Refer to prior CT dated approximately 2 months prior for comparison. LDH reported at 512 U/L at time of imaging.

TECHNIQUE:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Multidetector CT performed from the thoracic inlet through the pubic symphysis following the uneventful administration of 100 mL of nonionic iodinated intravenous contrast (iopamidol). Imaging acquired in the portal venous phase. Axial, coronal, and sagittal reformatted images reviewed. No acute allergic reaction to contrast noted. Oral contrast was not administered.

MRI brain: Multisequence MRI of the brain performed at 1.5 Tesla, including pre- and post-gadolinium sequences (axial T1, T2, FLAIR, DWI/ADC, and gadolinium-enhanced T1-weighted sequences in axial and coronal planes). 15 mL of gadobutrol administered without complication.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis from approximately 8 weeks prior (6-month restaging study). PET-CT from time of initial staging (approximately 8 months prior).

---

FINDINGS:

CT CHEST:

Pulmonary parenchyma: There are two new small nodular opacities within the right lower lobe, measuring approximately 6 mm and 4 mm in greatest dimension. These are smooth-bordered, subpleural in location, and demonstrate soft tissue attenuation without internal calcification or cavitation. They were not present on the prior comparison study. There is no left-sided pulmonary nodule. No consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. The airways are patent to the segmental level bilaterally.

Mediastinum and hila: The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The mediastinal and hilar contours are unremarkable. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes by size criteria. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber.

Chest wall and soft tissues: The primary surgical site at the left posterior shoulder demonstrates expected post-excisional changes without evidence of local recurrence identified on this study. No new subcutaneous nodularity identified in the chest wall or pectoral regions.

Axillae and supraclavicular regions: There is interval and notable enlargement of a right axillary lymph node, now measuring approximately 16 mm in short axis (previously 5 mm on prior study). This node demonstrates heterogeneous enhancement following contrast administration and a rounded, rather than reniform, morphology, raising concern for nodal metastatic involvement. Additionally, a new left axillary lymph node measuring approximately 11 mm in short axis has appeared since the prior study, demonstrating similar morphologic features. The bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy had previously shown excellent and progressive response; this interval growth is a notable and unfavorable change.

Bones (chest): No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesions identified within the imaged thoracic spine or ribs. No pathologic fractures.

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver: The liver demonstrates heterogeneous parenchymal enhancement. There has been clear and substantial interval growth of previously identified metastatic hepatic lesions compared to the prior restaging study. In the right hepatic lobe, a hypodense lesion previously measuring approximately 10 mm now measures approximately 23 mm, representing roughly a doubling in size. A second right lobe lesion previously measuring approximately 5 mm now measures approximately 14 mm and has become conspicuous. In addition, there is a new focus of hypodensity within the right hepatic lobe measuring approximately 8 mm that was not identified on prior imaging; this is strongly suspected to represent a new metastatic deposit given the clinical context, though at this size distinction from a benign hepatic cyst cannot be made on CT alone -- correlation with prior imaging confirms this is a new finding. The left hepatic lobe lesions previously documented had shown near-complete resolution and remain not measurably visible on the current study, which is an area of maintained treatment effect. Notably, however, the overall hepatic tumor burden has markedly increased compared to the 6-month study.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal lesions.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. Normal pancreatic ductal caliber. No peripancreatic fluid collections.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma with acquired resistance to first-line dabrafenib/trametinib -- DISEASE PROGRESSION

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo has developed progressive disease after approximately 8 months on dabrafenib/trametinib combination therapy. This is a challenging but anticipated clinical development. Her initial response was impressive -- approximately 68% reduction at best response -- and the duration of control (8 months from start) is within the range expected for this regimen in patients with high-burden, BRAF-mutant melanoma.

Mechanistically, acquired resistance to BRAFi/MEKi is multifactorial. In her case, the PTEN p.R130Q mutation identified at baseline is relevant -- PTEN loss activates PI3K/AKT signaling, which can serve as a bypass mechanism to MAPK pathway inhibition and has been linked to reduced durability of response. Additionally, CDKN2A homozygous deletion may contribute to cell cycle dysregulation independent of BRAF pathway blockade.

Given the clear progression on BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy, and considering her overall molecular profile -- BRAF V600E-mutant with high TMB (42 mutations/Mb) and PD-L1 TPS of 45% -- she is an excellent candidate for checkpoint inhibitor therapy. There is strong evidence from CheckMate-067 and subsequent long-term follow-up data supporting the combination of ipilimumab plus nivolumab as a highly active regimen in metastatic melanoma, including in patients with prior BRAF-targeted therapy. In the sequencing debate, her high TMB and meaningful PD-L1 expression suggest solid potential for durable immunotherapy benefit.

Plan: Initiate ipilimumab (Yervoy) 1 mg/kg IV plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 induction doses, per the CheckMate-067 lower-dose ipilimumab schema. This regimen is chosen to balance efficacy with an intention to reduce immune-related toxicity burden compared to the standard 3 mg/kg ipi / 1 mg/kg nivo dosing, particularly relevant given her hepatic involvement and prior hepatic transaminase elevations. Following 4 induction doses, plan to transition to nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as flat-dose maintenance. Standard pre-medications (diphenhydramine, acetaminophen) will be given with each infusion.

Dabrafenib and trametinib will be discontinued today. There is no role for continued targeted therapy in the setting of clear progression, and there are washout considerations prior to immunotherapy initiation. We will allow approximately 1 week of washout before starting first immunotherapy infusion.

2. Immune-Related Adverse Event Risk Counseling

An extensive counseling session was conducted today with Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo and her partner, who accompanied her to this visit. We discussed the following in detail:
  - Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) are a significant class effect of checkpoint inhibitor therapy and differ fundamentally from the side effects of chemotherapy or targeted therapy.
  - The most common serious irAEs include immune-mediated colitis/diarrhea, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies (thyroiditis, adrenal insufficiency, hypophysitis), and dermatitis.
  - With combination ipi/nivo, the rate of grade 3-4 irAEs is approximately 55-60% in published trials, substantially higher than with nivolumab alone.
  - She was counseled to contact our clinic immediately or go to the emergency department if she develops: more than 3-4 loose bowel movements per day above her baseline (or any bloody diarrhea), fever, skin rash, shortness of breath, significant fatigue, or visual changes.
  - Patient was provided with an immunotherapy toxicity wallet card and our after-hours contact number.
  - She verbalized clear understanding of the warning signs and agreed to present promptly for evaluation.
  - Steroid use for irAEs and its implications (including risk of adrenal insufficiency with prolonged use) were discussed.
  - Specifically regarding her prior hepatic transaminase elevations, she was told that immune hepatitis can occur and we will be monitoring LFTs closely -- every 2-3 weeks during induction.

Prior to each immunotherapy infusion: CBC with differential, CMP (specifically AST, ALT, total bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, creatinine), LDH, and thyroid function (TSH with reflex free T4) every 4-6 weeks.

Will continue to monitor. Baseline pulse oximetry at rest is 97% on room air -- reassuring. She understands this is a next-line regimen and that her disease course has been challenging, but she remains engaged and optimistic. Her partner is a strong support.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma, previously treated with dabra/tram (first-line, with partial response and subsequent progression at approximately 8 months), now on second-line Yervoy/nivo combination immunotherapy, presenting with grade 3 immune-mediated colitis after cycle 3, now on hospital day 4 with clear clinical improvement on high-dose IV methylprednisolone.

PROBLEM 1: GRADE 3 IMMUNE-MEDIATED (CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR-ASSOCIATED) COLITIS

The diagnosis of grade 3 irAE colitis is well-established in this case. The clinical picture, endoscopic appearance, histopathology (immune-infiltrate colitis pattern without infectious etiology), and negative infectious workup all point firmly to immune checkpoint inhibitor-induced colitis. The severe presentation with 10-12 bloody stools/day (grade 3 per CTCAE v5.0) and CRP of 94 mg/L on admission required immediate drug hold and initiation of high-dose systemic corticosteroids per ASCO and ESMO immune toxicity guidelines.

- Corticosteroids: She is responding very well to IV methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg/day (currently day 4 of treatment). Stool frequency has reduced from 10-12 to 4-5 stools/day, with resolution of gross blood and significant pain improvement. CRP has fallen from 94 to 24 mg/L. This trajectory is reassuring.
  - Plan: Continue IV methylprednisolone for a total of approximately 3-5 days IV, then transition to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (approximately 65 mg daily) once stool frequency is ≤3/day with no blood for 24 hours. I anticipate transitioning tomorrow or the day after.
  - Planned oral prednisone taper: Very gradual taper over 6-8 weeks minimum per ASCO immune toxicity guidelines — rapid steroid taper in this setting is associated with colitis flare and relapse. We will taper by approximately 10-20% every 1-2 weeks, guided by symptoms.
  - If she fails to continue improving or worsens over the next 24-48 hours, we should have a low threshold to add infliximab (5 mg/kg IV) or vedolizumab as second-line immunosuppression — I discussed this with gastroenterology and they are in agreement. At this time, her trajectory does not require this escalation.
- GI prophylaxis: Continue omeprazole throughout steroid course. Calcium 500 mg BID and vitamin D 800 IU daily added today to mitigate steroid-induced bone density loss.
- Steroid-induced hyperglycemia: Glucose 164 mg/dL today — nursing to continue glucose monitoring with meals and at bedtime. If consistently >200 mg/dL, will involve endocrinology/diabetes management service for correction dosing.
- Bowel rest: Low-residue diet as tolerated; IV fluids weaning as oral intake improves.
- Colonoscopy: Not required at this time; flexible sigmoidoscopy with biopsy was diagnostic. May consider follow-up colonoscopy after clinical remission to confirm mucosal healing prior to any immunotherapy rechallenge, per gastroenterology's recommendation.

PROBLEM 2: ipi — PERMANENT DISCONTINUATION

Given grade 3 immune-mediated colitis, ipi is permanently discontinued per ASCO guidelines and our institutional protocol. This is a non-negotiable decision. I discussed this clearly with the patient and her family today. Grade 3 colitis on ipi mandates permanent cessation of the anti-CTLA-4 agent; rechallenge carries unacceptable risk of recurrent and potentially life-threatening toxicity including perforation and toxic megacolon.

PROBLEM 3: Opdivo RECHALLENGE — PLAN FOR FUTURE CONSIDERATION

Opdivo is currently held. The question of rechallenge with anti-PD-1 monotherapy after grade 3 colitis is a nuanced clinical decision. In this patient's case, there are several factors strongly favoring eventual Opdivo rechallenge:
- Tumor is demonstrably responding to immunotherapy (ongoing CT response, LDH trending down)
- High TMB (42 mut/Mb) and PD-L1 TPS 45% suggest strong immunotherapy sensitivity
- The mechanism of anti-PD-1 and anti-CTLA-4 toxicities differ somewhat, and published data suggest anti-PD-1 rechallenge after CTLA-4-related grade 3 colitis is feasible in selected patients with acceptable re-toxicity rates
- Published case series and retrospective data support rechallenge with anti-PD-1 after steroid management in patients who achieve complete resolution of colitis

Plan: Rechallenge with Opdivo monotherapy 480 mg IV every 4 weeks is planned after the following criteria are met:
  1. Complete clinical resolution of colitis (stool frequency ≤1-2 per day, no blood, no cramping)
  2. Complete or near-complete steroid taper (prednisone dose ≤10 mg/day)
  3. Endoscopic confirmation of mucosal healing strongly preferred (per gastroenterology)
  4. CRP normalized
  This decision will not be made for at minimum 8-12 weeks. I have communicated this timeline clearly to the patient. She understands and accepts the plan.

PROBLEM 4: ONCOLOGIC STATUS — MELANOMA

Incidentally, the CT obtained on admission shows ongoing partial response of the hepatic and nodal disease to Yervoy/nivo, despite only 3 cycles having been administered. This is a positive signal confirming immunotherapy activity in this case. LDH is trending down from its peak of 512 U/L at progression on BRAFi/MEKi to currently 312 U/L. Although we are managing a significant toxicity, the tumor biology appears to be responding favorably, which further supports the plan for Opdivo maintenance rechallenge after toxicity resolution.

No new anti-cancer therapy to be initiated during this acute hospitalization.

PROBLEM 5: HYPOTHYROIDISM / IMMUNE-RELATED ENDOCRINOPATHY MONITORING

Baseline hypothyroidism is present (pre-existing, on levothyroxine 75 mcg). TSH today 3.4 mIU/L on current dose. Free T4 low-normal. Checkpoint inhibitors — particularly anti-PD-1 agents — carry a well-known risk of immune-mediated thyroiditis and hypothyroidism, either worsening pre-existing disease or de novo. We will monitor thyroid function closely with repeat TSH and free T4 at discharge and at first outpatient follow-up. Endocrinology is available for comanagement if thyroid abnormalities worsen.

PROBLEM 6: ANEMIA / NUTRITIONAL STATUS

Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL — mild anemia in the context of GI blood loss and chronic inflammatory state. Iron studies to be obtained. If iron deficient, oral iron supplementation after GI inflammation resolves. No indication for transfusion at this time. Albumin mildly low (3.4 g/dL); dietitian consultation placed for nutritional support. Multivitamin added.

DISPOSITION

Medically improving. Anticipate transition to oral steroids tomorrow (hospital day 5) if stool frequency continues to improve. Plan for discharge on hospital day 5-6 once she is tolerating oral prednisone, oral intake is adequate, vital signs are stable, and she has received patient education on steroid taper schedule, signs of colitis relapse (bloody diarrhea, >4 BMs/day, cramping, fever), and warning signs requiring return to the emergency department.

DISCHARGE PLANNING / FOLLOW-UP

1. Oncology outpatient follow-up within 2 weeks of discharge to reassess clinical status and steroid taper compliance
2. Gastroenterology follow-up in 4-6 weeks for clinical reassessment; colonoscopy scheduling to assess mucosal healing before Opdivo rechallenge
3. Endocrinology referral placed for thyroid monitoring given ongoing anti-PD-1 therapy (pending)
4. Primary care physician to be updated regarding current hospitalization, steroid therapy, and implications for long-term management
5. Dietitian consultation before discharge

PATIENT EDUCATION

I spent approximately 30 minutes with Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo and her husband at bedside today reviewing:
- What happened: immune system over-activation caused by ipi/Opdivo attacking the colon lining — this is an expected but serious side effect of this class of medication
- Why ipi is being permanently stopped (non-negotiable; too dangerous to rechallenge)
- The reasoning for eventually rechallenging with nivo monotherapy and why this is in her interest
- Importance of not stopping prednisone abruptly (risk of adrenal insufficiency and colitis flare)
- When to return to ED: >4-5 stools/day, blood in stool, severe cramping, fever, or inability to take oral medications
- Encouraging news regarding tumor response seen on imaging

The patient verbalized understanding of the plan and expressed her desire to continue treatment when safe to do so. She appeared emotionally resilient and appropriately engaged. Her husband was supportive and asked thoughtful questions.

Overall, this patient is navigating a serious treatment complication but is on a favorable clinical trajectory. The underlying melanoma appears to be responding to immunotherapy, and I remain cautiously optimistic that controlled nivo rechallenge after complete toxicity resolution will provide durable disease control in this high-TMB, PD-L1-positive patient.

Cancer status: Present, with active disease — unable to formally assess treatment response in the context of acute immune toxicity management; however, incidental CT findings during this admission are consistent with ongoing response to prior immunotherapy.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with a history of hypothyroidism (previously on levothyroxine 75 mcg) and mixed Afro-Caribbean/Eastern European descent who was diagnosed with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant cutaneous melanoma originating from the left posterior shoulder (Breslow thickness 4.2 mm, ulcerated, Clark level V, 4 mitoses/mm2, lymphovascular invasion present) with metastases to bilateral axillary lymph nodes, multiple subcutaneous nodules, and four hepatic lesions at initial presentation. Her LDH at diagnosis was elevated at 387 U/L.

She was initially treated with first-line dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg daily, achieving a significant partial response (roughly 68% reduction in measurable disease) by six months with normalization of LDH. This was complicated by two episodes of drug-related pyrexia — the second of which (temperature 39.1°C with rigors) necessitated a dabrafenib dose reduction to 100 mg BID — as well as grade 1 hand-foot syndrome and grade 2 peripheral edema. Despite initial excellent response, repeat CT imaging at approximately eight months demonstrated clear progression: enlarging axillary lymphadenopathy, growth and a new hepatic lesion, and two new small pulmonary nodules in the right lower lobe. Given acquired resistance to BRAF/MEK inhibition and her favorable tumor immunologic profile (PD-L1 45%, TMB 42 mut/Mb), she was transitioned to second-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab 1 mg/kg plus nivolumab 3 mg/kg IV every three weeks (CheckMate-067 dosing schema). She was admitted to the hospital, infectious etiologies were thoroughly excluded, and flexible sigmoidoscopy confirmed diffuse moderate-to-severe colitis from the rectum to the descending colon. She was treated with IV methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg/day with good response, transitioned to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (80 mg daily), and discharged on hospital day 5. Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 56-year-old woman with a background of Stage IV (M1c) superficial spreading cutaneous melanoma arising from the left posterior shoulder, initially diagnosed approximately 18 months ago. COMMENT:

The histomorphologic and immunophenotypic findings in this core biopsy are consistent with complete pathologic response (pCR) at this hepatic site. The discrete zone of dense, mature collagenous fibrosis with associated hemosiderin deposition and pigment-laden macrophages without viable melanoma cells is a well-recognized pattern of treatment-induced tumor regression in melanoma following immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. The complete absence of immunoreactivity for HMB-45, Melan-A, and SOX10 — all sensitive and specific melanocytic markers — and the absence of S100-positive cells with melanoma morphology support the interpretation of no residual viable melanoma.

The focal S100 positivity observed is confined to reactive stromal elements (dendritic cells, portal nerve fibers), a pattern commonly encountered in regressed or necrotic tumor beds and not indicative of viable melanoma.

The degree of background hepatic fibrosis (F1) and mild steatosis are noted as incidental findings in the uninvolved parenchyma, likely related to prior treatment effects (BRAFi/MEKi exposure, systemic inflammation from immune-mediated colitis) and/or metabolic factors.

This case was reviewed and signed out by the staff pathologist. Correlation with clinical and radiographic findings is recommended, as always.

Ms. Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 57-year-old female with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma, now presenting for follow-up review of recent imaging and liver biopsy results. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo has had a remarkable and complicated treatment course over the past approximately two years since her initial diagnosis of Stage IV melanoma. To briefly summarize the relevant history:

She was originally diagnosed with BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma arising from a left posterior shoulder primary (4.2 mm Breslow, Clark level V, ulcerated) with synchronous metastases to bilateral axillary lymph nodes, multiple right flank subcutaneous nodules, and four hepatic lesions (largest 3.1 cm) confirmed on staging PET-CT. Comprehensive NGS demonstrated BRAF V600E (VAF 48%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, PTEN R130Q (VAF 31%), TP53 R248W, and high TMB at 42 mut/Mb; PD-L1 TPS was 45% by IHC.

Given the rapid clinical trajectory and hepatic disease burden with elevated LDH, she was initiated on first-line dabra 150 mg BID plus tram 2 mg daily. She had an early, striking response — the 8-week restaging CT showed an approximately 62% reduction in target lesion burden with near-complete resolution of hepatic and subcutaneous disease. The 6-month restaging similarly showed continued partial response with approximately 68% overall reduction. However, at approximately 8 months, she developed acquired resistance to the BRAFi/MEKi doublet with progressive hepatic disease, new bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, and new pulmonary nodules, with LDH rising to 512 U/L. Her disease course on targeted therapy was complicated by two episodes of Tafinlar-related pyrexia (the second requiring a brief ED visit and subsequent dose reduction of Tafinlar to 100 mg BID), as well as grade 1 rash and hand-foot syndrome.

Given progression on targeted therapy, she was transitioned to second-line ipi 1 mg/kg plus Opdivo 3 mg/kg Q3W (CheckMate-067 dosing schema). Her course was complicated by grade 3 immune-mediated colitis during cycle 3, requiring hospitalization, high-dose IV methylprednisolone transitioning to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with slow taper, and permanent discontinuation of ipi. She recovered well with the steroid taper; flexible sigmoidoscopy confirmed colitis resolution prior to Opdivo rechallenge. Despite the toxicity, her initial imaging at approximately 10 weeks from combination initiation (during the steroid taper) already demonstrated a partial response — hepatic lesions and axillary nodes significantly reduced. She was then rechallenged on nivo monotherapy 480 mg IV Q4W as maintenance.

On nivo maintenance, she developed immune-related hypothyroidism (TSH peaking at 9.2 mIU/L, free T4 0.7 ng/dL), managed with sequential levothyroxine dose uptitrations to 125 mcg daily. Serial restaging has shown progressive deepening of her response: at 6 months of nivo maintenance, hepatic lesions had diminished to 0.6 cm and 0.3 cm with pulmonary nodules no longer visualized; at 12 months of nivo maintenance, she achieved near-complete response with a single residual sub-centimeter right hepatic lesion measuring 0.4 cm, with LDH fully normalized at 162 U/L.

Given the near-complete radiographic response and the residual hepatic lesion's small size and stability, a multidisciplinary decision was made to perform an image-guided core needle biopsy of the residual right hepatic lesion to assess for treatment viability. This biopsy was performed by interventional radiology without significant complication. Results have now returned and are being discussed at today's visit.

Today, Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo reports feeling genuinely well. She has resumed full-time work as a landscape architect. She reports no abdominal pain, no right upper quadrant discomfort, no diarrhea (bowel habits normal at 1-2 formed stools daily, a significant improvement from her colitis nadir), and no dyspnea. She has not noticed any new skin lesions, lumps, or bumps. She is tolerating levothyroxine 125 mcg well with no symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism. She denies fever, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight changes. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo was originally diagnosed approximately 3 years ago with Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, M1c) cutaneous malignant melanoma arising from the left posterior shoulder, presenting with a 4.2 mm Breslow thickness, Clark level V primary lesion with ulceration. At initial staging, she had bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, multiple subcutaneous nodules, and four hepatic metastases with the largest measuring 3.1 cm. LDH was elevated at 387 U/L at diagnosis. She was initially treated with Tafinlar 150 mg BID plus Mekinist 2 mg daily, achieving a deep partial response — approximately 68% tumor burden reduction at best response — but experienced progression at approximately 8 months, with enlarging axillary adenopathy, growing hepatic lesions, and new small pulmonary nodules. LDH rose to 512 U/L at that time. She was transitioned to second-line ipi 1 mg/kg plus nivo 3 mg/kg (CheckMate-067 dosing) every 3 weeks.

During cycle 3 of the ipi/nivo combination, she developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis requiring hospitalization, high-dose IV methylprednisolone, and permanent discontinuation of Yervoy. Notably, her tumors responded to the immunotherapy combination — post-hospitalization restaging imaging demonstrated a partial response. After completing the steroid taper and confirmatory flexible sigmoidoscopy showing colitis resolution, she was rechallenged on nivo 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance. Over approximately 18 months of nivo maintenance, she achieved a deepening and ultimately near-complete radiographic response. At 12 months of nivo maintenance (15 months from the start of combination immunotherapy), residual imaging showed only a single subcentimeter hepatic lesion (0.4 cm right lobe), and image-guided biopsy of that lesion demonstrated no viable melanoma — only fibrotic/necrotic tissue with pigment-laden macrophages, consistent with complete pathologic response at that site.

GROSS DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A (Left posterior shoulder -- WLE):
Received fresh, subsequently formalin-fixed, is an ellipse of skin and underlying subcutaneous soft tissue measuring 7.8 x 5.2 x 2.6 cm. The overlying epidermis bears a centrally located, irregularly pigmented macule-to-plaque lesion measuring 2.4 x 1.9 cm. The lesion is dark brown to black with focal areas of gray-white discoloration and an area of surface erosion/crusting measuring 0.5 x 0.3 cm centrally, consistent with ulceration. The surface of the lesion is flat to minimally elevated with irregular, asymmetric borders. Surrounding skin is unremarkable. The specimen is inked (peripheral margins blue, deep margin black) and serially sectioned perpendicular to the long axis at 3 mm intervals. On sectioning, the tumor invades deeply into the subcutaneous fat, with the leading edge at the deep resection margin measuring at least 4 mm clearance. The deepest extent of invasion corresponds grossly to the lower dermis/subcutaneous junction. No grossly apparent satellite nodules identified within the specimen. A total of 14 sections are submitted for histologic evaluation as follows:
  A1-A3: Full face sections through the geometric center of the lesion (perpendicularly sectioned)
  A4-A6: Sections through the thickest portion of the lesion with deep margin
  A7-A9: Lateral peripheral margins, left and right
  A10-A11: Superior and inferior peripheral margins
  A12: Additional sections of thickest area for immunohistochemistry
  A13-A14: Representative sections of uninvolved skin

Specimen B (Right axillary lymph node -- core needle biopsy):
Received in formalin are three tan-white core biopsy fragments, each measuring approximately 1.0 cm in length and 0.1-0.2 cm in diameter. Aggregate tissue: 3 cores. All tissue entirely submitted in one cassette.
  B1: All 3 cores entirely submitted

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A (Left posterior shoulder -- WLE):
Histologic sections reveal a malignant melanocytic neoplasm characterized by an asymmetric, poorly circumscribed proliferation of atypical melanocytes arranged in irregular nests and as individual cells extending along the dermal-epidermal junction (lentiginous and pagetoid patterns) as well as deeply invading the dermis and subcutaneous adipose tissue. The overlying epidermis demonstrates focal full-thickness replacement by atypical melanocytes. The intraepidermal component shows a superficial spreading growth pattern, with confluent and discontinuous nests of melanocytes at the dermal-epidermal junction, along with prominent single-cell pagetoid migration extending to the granular layer in multiple foci. In areas, the intraepidermal component extends beyond the dermal invasive component by several millimeters.

The dermal invasive component consists of sheets, fascicles, and irregular nests of epithelioid and focally spindled melanocytes with vesicular nuclei, prominent eosinophilic nucleoli, and moderate-to-abundant pale eosinophilic cytoplasm. Many cells contain fine to coarsely granular brown melanin pigment. Marked nuclear pleomorphism is present. Mitotic figures are readily identified, including atypical forms, with a count of 4 mitoses per mm2 (hot spot enumeration per CAP protocol). The tumor invades deeply through the reticular dermis and into the subcutaneous adipose tissue (Clark level V).

The Breslow thickness, measured from the top of the granular layer (or surface of ulceration) to the deepest invasive melanocyte, is 4.2 mm.

Ulceration: PRESENT. An area of surface ulceration measuring 0.5 mm in horizontal extent is identified, with overlying fibrinous exudate and neutrophilic infiltrate replacing the epidermis.

Tumor infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs): Brisk, nonbrisk pattern; a moderate band-like lymphocytic infiltrate is seen at the base of the tumor and focally infiltrating between tumor cells.

Regression: ABSENT. No areas of fibroplasia with melanophages replacing invasive tumor are identified.

Lymphovascular invasion: PRESENT. Multiple foci of neoplastic melanocytes are identified within vascular spaces in the reticular dermis, highlighted on D2-40 immunostaining (lymphovascular invasion confirmed; see ancillary studies).

Perineural invasion: ABSENT. No tumor cells identified in proximity to dermal nerves.

Satellite nodules: ABSENT.

Surgical margins:
  - Deep margin: Uninvolved. Closest deep margin: 4 mm (measured in A4).
  - Peripheral margins: All uninvolved. All peripheral margins are greater than 5 mm from the nearest tumor cell (closest peripheral margin: approximately 6 mm, lateral margin in A7).

Background skin: Scattered melanocytic nevi not identified in the adjacent sections. No evidence of regression in surrounding skin.

Specimen B (Right axillary lymph node -- core needle biopsy):
Sections of the three core fragments reveal fibrofatty and fibrous tissue with fragments of lymph node parenchyma. Within the sampled lymph node tissue, there is replacement of a substantial portion of the nodal architecture by sheets and cohesive nests of epithelioid tumor cells with identical morphology to the primary cutaneous melanoma (vesicular nuclei, prominent nucleoli, abundant pale cytoplasm, intracytoplasmic melanin pigment). The tumor deposit measures at least 18 mm on the largest cross-section within the biopsy material (note: core biopsy only, full nodal architecture not entirely assessable). Extranodal extension is present: tumor nests are seen extending through the fibrous nodal capsule and into the perinodal adipose tissue in multiple foci. No normal residual lymph node follicles are identified in the involved area.

TIER I ALTERATIONS — Strong Clinical Significance
(Well-established clinical significance in this tumor type per FDA-approved labeling, NCCN, or professional society guidelines)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Gene: BRAF
Alteration: Missense mutation — p.Val600Glu (V600E); c.1799T>A; Exon 15
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 48%
Coverage at locus: 874x
Tier / Level of Evidence: Tier I — Level A (FDA-approved companion diagnostic indication)
Functional Consequence: Activating mutation resulting in constitutive BRAF kinase activation, independent of upstream RAS signaling. The V600E substitution introduces a glutamic acid residue in place of valine at codon 600, mimicking the phosphorylated activation loop and driving sustained MAPK/ERK pathway signaling and proliferation.
Clinical Interpretation: BRAF V600E is the most prevalent oncogenic driver in cutaneous melanoma, present in approximately 40-50% of cases. This alteration confers sensitivity to BRAF inhibitors (vemurafenib, dabrafenib) and BRAF/MEK inhibitor combination regimens (dabrafenib + trametinib, vemurafenib + cobimetinib, encorafenib + binimetinib). Per NCCN guidelines, this is a mandatory biomarker for systemic therapy selection in Stage IV melanoma.
Notable: NRAS wild-type and NF1 wild-type confirmed (see below), which supports a primary BRAF-driven oncogenic mechanism.

Gene: CDKN2A
Alteration: Homozygous deletion, chromosome 9p21.3; encompasses exons 1α, 1β, 2, and 3 (complete gene loss)
Copy Number: <0.5 (biallelic loss confirmed by normalized read depth and SNP-based allelic imbalance analysis)
Tier / Level of Evidence: Tier I — Level A
Functional Consequence: CDKN2A encodes two critical tumor suppressor proteins via alternative reading frames: p16/INK4A (CDK4/6 inhibitor, G1 cell cycle checkpoint) and p14/ARF (MDM2 regulator, p53 stabilizer). Homozygous deletion simultaneously abrogates both p16-RB axis-mediated G1 arrest and p14-mediated p53 stabilization, resulting in unchecked cell cycle progression and impaired apoptosis signaling. This represents one of the most common genomic alterations in melanoma, occurring in 40-60% of cases and enriched in metastatic disease.
Clinical Interpretation: Homozygous CDKN2A deletion is associated with more aggressive tumor behavior and may confer relative resistance to CDK4/6 inhibitor monotherapy (as the cell cycle is already unlocked at the CDK4/6 checkpoint via CDKN2A loss). This alteration, in combination with BRAF V600E, defines a particularly aggressive genomic subtype. It is also a potential germline susceptibility gene; germline assessment is recommended if personal/family history of familial atypical mole-melanoma (FAMM) syndrome is present.
Note: Germline CDK4 wild-type confirmed (see germline section).

Gene: PTEN
Alteration: Missense mutation — p.Arg130Gln (R130Q); c.389G>A; Exon 5 (phosphatase domain)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 31%
Coverage at locus: 912x
Loss of heterozygosity at PTEN locus: Detected (consistent with functional biallelic inactivation)
Tier / Level of Evidence: Tier I — Level A
Functional Consequence: The R130Q variant disrupts the catalytic phosphatase domain of PTEN, impairing its ability to dephosphorylate PIP3 to PIP2 and thereby causing constitutive activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling axis. Notably, R130Q is a well-characterized loss-of-function PTEN allele that retains partial protein structure but loses enzymatic activity.
Clinical Interpretation: PTEN loss/mutation is present in approximately 20-30% of metastatic melanomas and frequently co-occurs with BRAF V600E. Activation of the PI3K pathway via PTEN loss has been associated with reduced sensitivity to MEK inhibitor therapy in preclinical models, and may contribute to acquired resistance to BRAFi/MEKi combinations through MAPK-independent survival signaling. PTEN loss has also been associated with reduced response to anti-PD-1 immunotherapy in some studies, though the clinical impact remains incompletely defined in the context of high TMB and elevated PD-L1. This finding warrants monitoring for early resistance in the context of BRAF-targeted therapy.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TIER II ALTERATIONS — Potential Clinical Significance
(Clinical significance in this tumor type based on evidence from clinical trials or professional society guidelines; may inform clinical decision-making)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Gene: TP53
Alteration: Missense mutation — p.Arg248Trp (R248W); c.742C>T; Exon 7 (DNA-binding domain)
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 39%
Coverage at locus: 1,024x
Tier / Level of Evidence: Tier II — Level B
Functional Consequence: The R248W substitution is among the most frequently observed hotspot gain-of-function mutations in TP53. This residue directly contacts DNA; substitution with tryptophan abolishes sequence-specific DNA binding, resulting in loss of wild-type tumor suppressor activity. The R248W allele additionally confers gain-of-function oncogenic activities, including enhanced cell migration, invasion, and transcriptional reprogramming of proliferative gene networks. High VAF (39%) and probable loss of heterozygosity at the TP53 locus suggest biallelic disruption.
Clinical Interpretation: TP53 mutations are less common in primary melanoma (~20%) compared to other solid tumors but can be enriched in metastatic, UV-signature lesions. This alteration is consistent with the UV mutagenesis signature expected in cutaneous melanoma. R248W gain-of-function may contribute to chemotherapy resistance pathways, though cytotoxic chemotherapy is not a standard-of-care approach in this disease context. There are no currently approved targeted agents directly addressing TP53 GOF mutations; MDM2 inhibitors and mutant p53 reactivators (APR-246/eprenetapopt) remain investigational in melanoma. Monitoring and correlation with clinical behavior is warranted.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
TIER III ALTERATIONS — Uncertain Clinical Significance
(Variants of uncertain or limited clinical significance in this tumor type; may be relevant in investigational or research contexts)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Gene: RAC1
Alteration: Missense mutation — p.Pro29Ser (P29S); c.85C>T; Exon 2
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 12%
Coverage at locus: 687x
Tier / Level of Evidence: Tier III
Functional Consequence: RAC1 P29S is a recurrent hotspot mutation in melanoma, reported in approximately 5-9% of cutaneous melanoma cases with UV-signature mutagenesis (C>T transition at CC dinucleotide, characteristic of UV damage). This substitution renders RAC1 constitutively active in the GTP-bound state, activating downstream effectors including PAK1 and promoting cytoskeletal remodeling and cell proliferation. The relatively low VAF (12%) may represent a subclonal event.
Clinical Interpretation: RAC1 P29S is an emerging oncogenic driver in a subset of melanomas. There are no FDA-approved agents targeting RAC1 directly; PAK1 inhibitors are in early-phase clinical investigation. This alteration may contribute to the oncogenic landscape of this tumor and warrants consideration in the context of clinical trial eligibility.

Gene: PREX2
Alteration: Frameshift insertion — p.Thr935fs (c.2803_2804insT); Exon 20
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 22%
Coverage at locus: 543x
Tier / Level of Evidence: Tier III
Functional Consequence: PREX2 encodes a guanine nucleotide exchange factor (GEF) that activates RAC1 and also functions as a binding partner and negative regulator of PTEN. Frameshift mutations in PREX2 are among the most recurrently mutated non-hotspot alterations in melanoma (approximately 14% of cutaneous melanomas carry PREX2 somatic mutations). This frameshift is predicted to result in loss of the C-terminal PTEN-interacting domain, potentially further dysregulating PTEN tumor suppressor activity in the context of the co-occurring PTEN R130Q mutation identified above. Functional synergy between PREX2 loss-of-function and PTEN mutation may amplify PI3K/AKT signaling beyond what either alteration alone would produce.
Clinical Interpretation: No approved therapies target PREX2 directly. This finding, in conjunction with the PTEN mutation and the known regulatory relationship between PREX2 and PTEN, suggests compounded loss of PI3K pathway regulation. This context may be relevant for future PI3K/AKT/mTOR-directed investigational therapies.

Gene: ARID2
Alteration: Nonsense mutation — p.Gln444* (Q444*); c.1330C>T; Exon 9
Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 19%
Coverage at locus: 612x
Tier / Level of Evidence: Tier III
Functional Consequence: ARID2 encodes a subunit of the PBAF (Polybromo-associated BAF) chromatin remodeling complex. Loss-of-function mutations in ARID2 disrupt chromatin accessibility regulation, which can alter gene expression programs involved in differentiation, cell cycle control, and immune evasion. ARID2 is the second most commonly mutated SWI/SNF component in melanoma, with somatic mutations in approximately 7-12% of cases, predominantly generated by UV-induced C>T transitions.
Clinical Interpretation: ARID2 loss has been associated with altered enhancer accessibility in melanoma cells and may contribute to transcriptional dysregulation promoting tumor progression. Preclinical studies suggest potential synthetic lethality with EZH2 inhibitors (in SWI/SNF-deficient tumors), though this has not been validated in melanoma clinical trials. No approved targeted therapies are directly indicated for ARID2 loss at this time.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TMB Result:    42 mutations/Mb
Classification: HIGH (TMB-High threshold: ≥10 mutations/Mb)
Interpretation: This tumor demonstrates a markedly elevated tumor mutational burden consistent with UV-induced mutagenesis (C>T transitions at dipyrimidine sites), which is the dominant mutational signature in cutaneous melanoma. High TMB (>10 mut/Mb) is an FDA-approved companion biomarker for pembrolizumab (Keytruda) in the tumor-agnostic setting (KEYNOTE-158 approval). In the context of melanoma specifically, high TMB has been associated with increased neoantigen burden and potentially enhanced response to immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy. The observed TMB of 42 mut/Mb places this tumor in the upper quartile for cutaneous melanoma (median TMB in cutaneous melanoma: ~15-17 mut/Mb).

Dominant mutational signature analysis: C>T transitions predominate, consistent with COSMIC Signature 7 (UV light exposure), as expected for a cutaneous melanoma arising in a patient with significant sun exposure history. Signature 7 accounts for approximately 78% of observed substitutions. Secondary contribution from Signature 1 (age-related spontaneous deamination) also detected.

Loci assessed: >1,800 microsatellite loci across the genome
Interpretation: No microsatellite instability detected. The high TMB observed in this tumor is attributable to UV-induced mutagenesis rather than mismatch repair (MMR) deficiency. MSS status in the context of high TMB is characteristic of cutaneous melanoma and does not confer the same degree of pan-tumor MSI-H-directed pembrolizumab predictive biomarker indication; however, the high TMB independently supports immunotherapy sensitivity assessment.

CLINICAL GENOMIC SUMMARY AND THERAPEUTIC IMPLICATIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

This tumor demonstrates a genomic profile consistent with UV-induced cutaneous melanoma and reveals a complex oncogenic landscape with implications for therapeutic selection:

1. BRAF-TARGETED THERAPY (Tier I Indication)
   BRAF V600E positivity (VAF 48%, confirmed) supports use of BRAF inhibitor monotherapy or BRAF+MEK inhibitor combination therapy (dabrafenib + trametinib, vemurafenib + cobimetinib, or encorafenib + binimetinib). These combinations are FDA-approved as first-line therapy in BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV melanoma (COMBI-d/v, coBRIM, COLUMBUS trials).

   CAUTION — PTEN Loss and PI3K Pathway Activation: The co-occurring PTEN R130Q mutation (with LOH) and PREX2 frameshift may activate the PI3K/AKT pathway, providing a potential MAPK-independent survival pathway. Preclinical data suggest PTEN-loss tumors may develop acquired resistance to MEK inhibition more rapidly. Clinical vigilance for early radiographic progression is recommended during BRAFi/MEKi therapy.

2. IMMUNE CHECKPOINT INHIBITOR THERAPY (Tier I/II Indication)
   High TMB (42 mut/Mb) and PD-L1 TPS 45% collectively support strong potential benefit from anti-PD-1 or anti-CTLA-4 + anti-PD-1 combination immunotherapy. Per NCCN, checkpoint inhibitor therapy (nivolumab ± ipilimumab; pembrolizumab) is a Category 1 first-line option for Stage IV melanoma regardless of BRAF status. In patients with high tumor burden and/or LDH elevation, some data support considering BRAFi/MEKi first for rapid disease control followed by immunotherapy; in patients without these features, upfront immunotherapy may be preferred.

   Note on PTEN loss and immunotherapy: Several retrospective studies have associated PTEN loss with reduced T-cell infiltration and attenuated response to anti-PD-1 therapy. However, the magnitude of TMB (42 mut/Mb) and the UV-associated mutagenic signature may partially offset this immunosuppressive effect.

3. CDKN2A HOMOZYGOUS DELETION
   Reflects complete loss of G1 checkpoint control via p16/CDK4/RB axis and p14/MDM2/p53 axis. This finding, in combination with TP53 R248W gain-of-function, suggests profound cell cycle dysregulation. CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) are not expected to have significant single-agent activity in tumors with CDKN2A homozygous deletion, as the CDK4/6-p16 node is already disrupted. No direct therapeutic implication for currently approved melanoma agents.

4. TP53 R248W GAIN-OF-FUNCTION
   This variant may contribute to genomic instability and transcriptional reprogramming but does not currently direct a specific approved therapy. Investigational trials evaluating mutant p53 reactivation (e.g., eprenetapopt) or synthetic lethal approaches may be relevant in the future.

5. CLINICAL TRIAL ELIGIBILITY
   The molecular profile of this tumor (BRAF V600E, PTEN loss, CDKN2A deletion, high TMB, RAC1 P29S, MYC amplification) may support eligibility for several investigational trials, including:
   - PI3K/AKT/mTOR inhibitor combinations with BRAFi/MEKi
   - Adoptive cell therapy or tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) trials in high-TMB melanoma
   - Epigenetic modifier trials in SWI/SNF-altered tumors (ARID2 loss)
   - RAC1-pathway directed studies
   Molecular tumor board review is recommended.

Limitations:
- This assay is designed for somatic variant detection in FFPE tumor specimens. Rare false positives can result from FFPE artifact (particularly at low VAF). All reported variants passed quality filters and were manually reviewed by a board-certified molecular pathologist.
- The assay does not evaluate all possible genomic alterations. Variants outside targeted gene regions, large structural rearrangements not spanning captured sequences, and epigenetic alterations are not assessed.
- Tumor-only sequencing without a matched normal may have limited sensitivity for detecting low-frequency germline variants and may not perfectly distinguish somatic from germline alterations at low allele frequencies.
- Copy number alteration calls in highly rearranged tumor genomes may be affected by tumor ploidy; ploidy estimation was performed and incorporated into CNA calling.
