CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 55-year-old woman of mixed Afro-Caribbean and Eastern European descent who presents for new patient consultation regarding recently diagnosed Stage IV melanoma. She was referred by her dermatologic surgeon following excision of a primary left posterior shoulder lesion and subsequent workup confirming widespread metastatic disease.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo reports that she first noticed a changing pigmented lesion on her left posterior shoulder approximately 6-8 months prior to presentation. She describes it as a mole that had been present for many years but became progressively darker, irregular in border, and began to bleed intermittently with minor friction from clothing. She brought this to the attention of her primary care physician, who referred her urgently to dermatology. Punch biopsy was performed, and pathology confirmed invasive superficial spreading melanoma. She subsequently underwent wide local excision of the primary lesion and a core needle biopsy of a right axillary lymph node that had been noted to be enlarged on examination.

Final pathology of the primary lesion showed invasive superficial spreading melanoma with Breslow thickness of 4.2 mm, Clark level V, ulceration present, 4 mitoses/mm2, and lymphovascular invasion. The right axillary lymph node biopsy demonstrated metastatic melanoma with the largest deposit measuring 18 mm and extranodal extension. Immunohistochemistry confirmed S100+, SOX10+, HMB-45+, Melan-A+, Ki-67 approximately 35%.

Molecular testing via a comprehensive 523-gene solid tumor NGS panel was performed on the lymph node biopsy specimen. This identified a BRAF p.V600E mutation (VAF 48%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and a PTEN p.R130Q mutation (VAF 31%). Additional alterations included TP53 p.R248W and RAC1 p.P29S among others. Tumor mutational burden was high at 42 mutations/Mb; microsatellite status was stable (MSS). PD-L1 TPS on companion diagnostic IHC was 45%.

For staging, she underwent whole-body 18F-FDG PET-CT, which demonstrated hypermetabolic bilateral axillary lymph nodes (right 2.1 cm, SUVmax 11.4; left 1.8 cm, SUVmax 9.7), multiple hypermetabolic subcutaneous nodules in the right flank (up to 1.4 cm, SUVmax 6.8) and left axillary region, and four FDG-avid hepatic lesions (largest 3.1 cm in the right lobe, SUVmax 14.2). No brain, bone, or pulmonary metastases were identified. LDH at time of staging was 387 U/L, approximately 1.5 times the upper limit of normal. This establishes M1c disease (Stage IV, AJCC 8th edition).

She presents today reporting a rapidly growing subcutaneous nodule on the right flank that has become tender and more prominent over the past 2-3 weeks. She has noted palpable lumps in both armpits for the past month. She endorses approximately 8 lbs of unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months, which she attributes partly to decreased appetite, and ongoing mild fatigue that limits prolonged exertion but does not impact her activities of daily living. She denies fever, night sweats, jaundice, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, hemoptysis, dyspnea at rest, or neurological symptoms.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 55-year-old woman presenting with newly diagnosed Stage IV (M1c) metastatic cutaneous melanoma arising from the left posterior shoulder. She has multiple sites of active disease including bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, subcutaneous metastases, and hepatic metastases (four lesions, largest 3.1 cm). BRAF V600E mutation is confirmed on NGS. PD-L1 TPS is 45%. TMB is high (42 mut/Mb). LDH is elevated at 387 U/L, indicating high tumor burden and an adverse prognostic factor in this setting. ECOG PS is 1. She is a nonsmoker, has preserved end-organ function, and has no significant autoimmune history.

1. SYSTEMIC THERAPY -- INITIATION OF FIRST-LINE TARGETED THERAPY

Given the following considerations, first-line therapy with dabrafenib 150 mg orally twice daily plus trametinib 2 mg orally once daily (BRAFi/MEKi doublet) has been selected:

   a. BRAF V600E mutation confirmed: patient is eligible for BRAFi/MEKi targeted therapy.
   b. Rapid disease kinetics: the patient has a rapidly enlarging subcutaneous nodule, palpable bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, and four hepatic metastases, with LDH 1.5x ULN. The need for rapid tumor burden reduction favors starting with targeted therapy ahead of immunotherapy, as BRAFi/MEKi combinations typically achieve faster initial disease control.
   c. PTEN p.R130Q mutation identified on NGS: this alteration is associated with reduced sensitivity to MEK inhibition and possibly with PI3K/AKT pathway activation, and will be factored into surveillance for early progression and future treatment planning. It does not preclude initiation of this regimen but does inform prognosis.
   d. The COMBI-d and COMBI-v trial data support dabrafenib/trametinib as highly effective first-line therapy in BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma, with objective response rates exceeding 65-70% and median PFS of approximately 11-12 months.
   e. Immunotherapy (e.g., ipilimumab/nivolumab or pembrolizumab monotherapy) remains a viable second-line option and may offer durable benefit, particularly given the high TMB and PD-L1 expression. The sequencing question (targeted therapy first vs. immunotherapy first) was discussed with the patient; given disease kinetics and hepatic involvement, we favor targeted therapy as the initial approach, reserving checkpoint inhibitor therapy for subsequent lines.

   Prescriptions written today:
   - Dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg orally twice daily (approximately 12 hours apart, taken on an empty stomach)
   - Trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg orally once daily (with or without food, same time daily)

   Patient education provided regarding:
   - Pill schedule and adherence
   - Common side effects: fever/pyrexia (most common, up to 70% with this combination), fatigue, nausea, rash, photosensitivity, hand-foot syndrome, hyperglycemia
   - When to call: any fever >38 degrees C requires same-day notification and likely dose interruption of dabrafenib; skin rash with blistering or mucous membrane involvement (DRESS/SJS precaution)
   - Strict sun protection required; patient's occupational sun exposure was discussed in detail, and she was counseled to wear full-coverage protective clothing and broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen
   - Drug-drug interactions: avoid strong CYP3A4 inhibitors/inducers, proton pump inhibitors may alter dabrafenib absorption
   - Contraception: although patient's menopausal status was not directly asked, will confirm; if premenopausal, teratogenicity counseling required

2. RESTAGING IMAGING
   MRI brain with gadolinium ordered today as baseline (not yet obtained from prior PET-CT, which showed no intracranial disease, but MRI is more sensitive). CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast planned at approximately 8 weeks after starting therapy to assess early response.

3. LABS

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 55-year-old female with known Stage IV metastatic cutaneous melanoma, BRAF V600E-mutant. Prior imaging includes whole-body PET-CT at diagnosis demonstrating hypermetabolic bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, multiple subcutaneous nodules of the right flank and left axillary region, and four FDG-avid hepatic lesions.

TECHNIQUE: CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the uneventful intravenous administration of 100 mL of iohexol contrast material. Acquisitions were obtained in the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis and in the standard chest protocol. Multiplanar reconstructions were generated. MRI of the brain was performed on a 1.5T scanner with standard axial T1, T2, T2 FLAIR, DWI/ADC, susceptibility-weighted imaging, and post-gadolinium T1 sequences.

COMPARISON: Whole-body 18F-FDG PET-CT performed at initial staging workup.

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK (MRI BRAIN):

The cerebral and cerebellar parenchyma are unremarkable in signal characteristics. The cortical ribbon appears intact without focal signal abnormality on T2/FLAIR sequences. No areas of restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarct. No abnormal foci of susceptibility artifact to suggest microhemorrhage or calcification. Following gadolinium administration, there are no enhancing lesions within the brain parenchyma, the leptomeninges, or along the dural surfaces. The brainstem and posterior fossa structures appear normal. Ventricles are of normal caliber and configuration. No mass effect or midline shift. The visualized calvarium is intact. The pituitary gland is unremarkable.

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The airways are patent to the segmental level. The lung parenchyma demonstrates no focal consolidation, mass, or suspicious nodule. There are no pleural effusions. The lung bases are clear. The previously noted PET-CT findings did not include pulmonary lesions, and none are identified on the current examination.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinal and hilar contours are within normal limits. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes are identified. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. Aorta and great vessels are unremarkable.

Axillary Regions: There has been a striking and highly favorable interval change in the bilateral axillary lymph nodes compared to the staging PET-CT. On the right, the previously prominent hypermetabolic axillary lymph node, which measured approximately 2.1 cm in short axis at the time of staging, has dramatically decreased in size and now measures approximately 0.7 cm in short axis. This node, while still visible, has become much less conspicuous, with loss of the rounded morphology and restoration of a more oblate, fatty-hilum-containing configuration. On the left, the previously enlarged left axillary lymph node measuring 1.8 cm has similarly diminished markedly, now measuring approximately 0.5 cm. These findings are consistent with a robust treatment response within the axillary nodal stations bilaterally.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: No suspicious chest wall lesion or new soft tissue mass.

Bony Thorax: No destructive osseous lesion identified in the ribs, sternum, or thoracic spine.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver: There has been a marked and impressive interval decrease in the hepatic tumor burden since the staging examination. At initial staging, four FDG-avid hepatic lesions were identified, with the dominant lesion in the right lobe measuring 3.1 cm. On the current examination:

- Right hepatic lobe, dominant lesion: The previously 3.1 cm hypervascular lesion has dramatically decreased in size, now measuring approximately 1.2 cm. The lesion retains some residual peripheral enhancement but is markedly smaller, with a more hypodense, potentially partially necrotic center on portal venous phase imaging.

- Right hepatic lobe, second lesion: The previously 1.8 cm lesion has substantially regressed and now measures approximately 0.6 cm. It appears as a subtle hypodense focus with minimal enhancement.

- Left hepatic lobe lesions: The two previously described left lobe lesions, measuring 1.2 cm and 0.9 cm at staging, have undergone near-complete regression. The 1.2 cm lesion is now represented by a tiny sub-centimeter focus that approaches the limit of CT resolution, and the 0.9 cm lesion is either no longer definitively identified or has involuted to below the level of confident detection. No discrete enhancing focus is identified in this region on delayed sequences.

Overall, the aggregate hepatic tumor burden has substantially diminished, representing a profound treatment response across all hepatic metastatic sites. No new hepatic lesions are identified. The liver parenchyma otherwise appears homogeneous with no biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein is patent.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. No ductal dilatation or parenchymal lesion.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are within normal limits. No adrenal mass or nodule.

Kidneys and Ureters: Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and demonstrate symmetric and symmetric contrast enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal mass. Bilateral renal cysts not present.

Bowel: Small and large bowel loops are unremarkable in caliber and wall thickness. No evidence of obstruction or inflammatory change. Appendix is normal.

Pelvis: Uterus and ovaries are within normal limits for age. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No free pelvic fluid.

Retroperitoneum: No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No retroperitoneal mass.

SUBCUTANEOUS SOFT TISSUES:

Right Flank: The right flank subcutaneous nodule, which measured up to 1.4 cm in the largest dimension on the staging PET-CT and was clinically palpable at consultation, has regressed profoundly. On the current examination it measures approximately 0.3 cm and is barely perceptible as a tiny subcutaneous soft tissue density. It is now below the threshold of reliable clinical palpation. No other soft tissue nodules of the right flank are identified.

Left Axillary Subcutaneous Region: The previously noted subcutaneous nodules in the left axillary region have substantially involuted and are not reliably identified as discrete lesions on the current CT.

Remainder of evaluated soft tissues, including the upper back and posterior shoulder at the site of the excised primary lesion (with expected postsurgical changes), are unremarkable. No new subcutaneous nodule or suspicious soft tissue density is identified elsewhere.

BONE WINDOWS:

No lytic or blastic osseous lesion identified in the imaged skeleton. Vertebral body heights are maintained throughout the thoracic and lumbar spine.

IMPRESSION:

1. Markedly favorable interval change in all sites of known metastatic melanoma following approximately 8 weeks of dabrafenib/trametinib targeted therapy. The overall disease burden is dramatically reduced compared to the baseline staging PET-CT.

2. Bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy has regressed substantially, with the right axillary node decreasing from 2.1 cm to 0.7 cm and the left axillary node from 1.8 cm to 0.5 cm. Both nodes are substantially smaller and approaching a more normal morphology.

3. Hepatic metastatic burden has profoundly decreased across all four previously identified lesions. The dominant right lobe lesion has reduced from 3.1 cm to 1.2 cm. 4. The previously identified and palpable right flank subcutaneous nodule has regressed from 1.4 cm to approximately 0.3 cm, approaching resolution.

5. Previously noted left axillary subcutaneous nodules are not reliably identified, consistent with complete or near-complete regression at this site.

6. No new sites of metastatic disease are identified.

7. MRI brain: No evidence of intracranial metastases. No leptomeningeal disease.

These findings are consistent with a substantial and multifocal treatment response across all previously identified sites of metastatic disease. No new organ involvement is demonstrated.

MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Restaging Assessment — 8-Week Interval

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Ms. Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 55-year-old female with known Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma presenting for her 8-week restaging visit and response assessment after initiation of dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg twice daily plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg once daily. She reports marked symptomatic improvement.

---

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo was diagnosed approximately 8 weeks ago with Stage IV (M1c) cutaneous melanoma arising from the left posterior shoulder, with a 4.2 mm Breslow thickness primary lesion notable for ulceration, Clark level V, lymphovascular invasion, and 4 mitoses/mm2. At diagnosis, she had widespread metastatic disease including multiple subcutaneous nodules (right flank, left axilla), bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy, and four hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm in right lobe). NGS confirmed a BRAF p.V600E mutation (VAF 48%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and PTEN p.R130Q mutation. TMB was high at 42 mut/Mb. LDH at diagnosis was elevated at 387 U/L, consistent with high tumor burden. She was started on dabrafenib/trametinib approximately 3 weeks after diagnosis confirmation.

Since starting therapy, Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo reports a dramatic improvement in her overall sense of well-being. Notably, she states that the right flank subcutaneous nodule — which had been palpable and tender — is no longer palpable. She reports a weight gain of approximately 5 pounds over the past 8 weeks, which she attributes to improved appetite and resolution of her earlier malaise. Her energy level has improved substantially from the baseline; she is now ambulating without difficulty and returning to light activities around the house.

She did experience a drug-related pyrexia early in the course, approximately 3 weeks after starting therapy. She developed a fever of 38.4°C with mild chills. Dabrafenib was held for 3 days and she was managed with acetaminophen. She resumed dabrafenib at full dose (150 mg BID) without recurrence of fever at that time. She also developed a mild maculopapular rash on both forearms, which was managed successfully with topical triamcinolone 0.1% cream and has since resolved. She continues to note grade 1 fatigue, though this has improved compared to prior visits.

She denies any new rash, visual changes, chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, blood in stool, dysuria, headache, or focal neurological symptoms. She has had no recurrence of fever since the initial episode. She is currently compliant with both medications as prescribed, taking dabrafenib with food to minimize GI irritation and timing trametinib separately from dabrafenib as instructed.

Today she presents for review of restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain obtained earlier this week, as well as for interval laboratory assessment and clinical evaluation.

---

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**

- Constitutional: Fatigue, grade 1, improved. Weight gain +5 lbs since starting treatment. No fever, chills, or night sweats currently.
- Skin/integument: Previous maculopapular rash on forearms largely resolved with topical steroid. Mild dryness noted. No new cutaneous lesions reported by patient. No palmar-plantar erythema or blistering, though she notes mild tenderness of bilateral palms with friction (grade 1 hand-foot syndrome, discussed below).

**IMAGING RESULTS**

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (obtained 2 days prior, read by our institution's radiologist):

- Right flank subcutaneous nodule: Now measuring 0.3 cm, barely perceptible — was 1.4 cm at baseline. Essentially resolved.
- Bilateral axillary lymphadenopathy: Significantly diminished. Right axillary node now 0.7 cm (was 2.1 cm); left axillary node now 0.5 cm (was 1.8 cm). No longer appears frankly pathologic.
- Hepatic metastases: Dramatic interval decrease. The dominant right lobe lesion has shrunk from 3.1 cm to 1.2 cm. The second right lobe lesion decreased from 1.8 cm to 0.6 cm. The two left lobe lesions previously measuring 1.2 cm and 0.9 cm show near-complete resolution — barely residual signal, if at all. No new hepatic lesions.
- Lungs: No pulmonary nodules or pleural effusions identified.
- No new sites of metastatic disease.

MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast (obtained same day as CT):
- No evidence of intracranial metastases.

Overall impression: Remarkable response across all disease sites after 8 weeks of dabrafenib/trametinib. The hepatic burden has dramatically diminished and the subcutaneous and nodal disease has near-resolved. Estimated approximately 60-65% reduction in total disease volume across target sites. No new lesions have appeared.

---

**ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

**Diagnosis:** Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic cutaneous melanoma, superficial spreading type

**1. Treatment Response — Dabrafenib/Trametinib:**
Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo has had an excellent and clinically meaningful response to first-line BRAFi/MEKi therapy. The imaging findings are striking — the hepatic lesions have shrunk dramatically (dominant lesion down from 3.1 cm to 1.2 cm), the nodal disease has markedly regressed, and the subcutaneous nodule has disappeared. Her LDH has normalized from 387 to 198 U/L, a strong surrogate marker of response. Clinically, she feels significantly better, has gained weight, and her exam confirms resolution of previously palpable disease. This is a very gratifying response after 8 weeks.

We discussed that BRAF-targeted therapy often produces rapid, deep responses in BRAF V600E-mutant melanoma, but that acquired resistance is a known challenge, typically emerging at a median of around 9-12 months. We discussed the importance of strict adherence to both drugs. I reiterated the importance of sun protection given enhanced photosensitivity with BRAF inhibition. We also discussed that our plan will be to maintain current therapy and reassess at 16 weeks for the next restaging imaging set.

Plan: **Continue dabrafenib 150 mg BID and trametinib 2 mg daily at current doses.** No dose reduction warranted at this time.

**2. Toxicity Management:**

- *Prior pyrexia (grade 1-2):* Resolved with brief dabrafenib hold and acetaminophen. Patient counseled to check temperature at home if she feels feverish and to contact our team for any fever >38.0°C. She has been provided with a temperature log to bring to future visits. Importance of not ignoring fevers was reinforced.

- *Maculopapular rash (grade 1):* Largely resolved with topical triamcinolone. No active lesions on exam today. She should continue topical steroid as needed. Reinforced avoidance of sun exposure and use of broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen, as BRAF inhibitors can increase susceptibility to squamous cell carcinoma/keratoacanthoma — she has been referred to dermatology for a baseline full-body skin check.

- *Hand-foot syndrome (grade 1):* Mild bilateral palmar tenderness, no blistering or functional limitation. Recommended use of thick emollient creams (urea-based, e.g., Udderly Smooth or Eucerin Intensive Repair) to bilateral hands and feet twice daily. Recommended cushioned, well-fitting footwear. Avoid hot water immersion. Does not require dose modification at this grade. Will monitor closely.

- *Fatigue (grade 1):* Improved from baseline, consistent with disease response. Encouraged moderate exercise as tolerated, adequate sleep, and nutritious diet.

**3. LFT Normalization:**
Transaminase elevation at baseline (ALT 52, AST 48) attributed to hepatic metastatic burden has normalized with response (ALT 28, AST 24). Will continue to monitor LFTs with each visit, as hepatotoxicity is also a potential side effect of dabrafenib/trametinib.

**4. Hypothyroidism:**
TSH 2.3 mIU/L — well-controlled on current levothyroxine 75 mcg. No adjustment needed. Will recheck at next visit.

**5. Future Immunotherapy Sequencing:**
As discussed at the initial consult, the plan upon eventual progression on targeted therapy will be to transition to checkpoint inhibitor-based immunotherapy. Ms. Vasquez-Okonkwo has a PD-L1 TPS of 45% and very high TMB (42 mut/Mb), which are favorable biomarkers for immunotherapy. We discussed this as the anticipated second-line strategy when/if needed. For now, we are focused on maximizing the response to the current regimen. Patient acknowledges understanding.

**6. Dermatology Referral:**
Ordered. Priority given the need for baseline skin examination for surveillance of new primary melanomas and BRAF inhibitor-associated squamoproliferative lesions.

**7. Return to Care:**
Next oncology visit in approximately 8 weeks, with repeat restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (with contrast) and MRI brain at 16 weeks from treatment initiation (approximately 8 weeks from today). Will obtain labs (CBC, CMP, LDH, TSH) 2 weeks prior to next visit. Patient instructed to contact triage line immediately for fever >38°C, rash worsening, visual changes, severe headache, nausea/vomiting, or any other concerning symptoms.

**Cancer Status: Present — responding to therapy (excellent partial response at 8 weeks).**
